title: Safety Department Comprehensive Knowledge Base
description: Complete reference for safety officers, HSE managers, and safety coordinators on industrial/refinery construction sites. Covers OSHA regulations, permits, incident investigation, metrics, JSA/JHA, emergency response, training, programs, environmental compliance, audits, documentation, and common violations.
disclaimer: This knowledge base is a reference tool. Always verify current OSHA regulations at osha.gov. State-plan states may have more stringent requirements. Site-specific owner/operator requirements may exceed these minimums.

section_01_osha_regulations.title: OSHA Regulations for Construction (29 CFR 1926)
section_01_osha_regulations.authority: Occupational Safety and Health Administration, U.S. Department of Labor

general_duty_clause.text_summary: Employers must furnish a place of employment free from recognized hazards causing or likely to cause death or serious physical harm.
general_duty_clause.application: Used when no specific OSHA standard exists but a known hazard is present

1926.20.title: General Safety and Health Provisions
requirements: Employer must initiate and maintain accident prevention programs
Competent person required for frequent and regular inspections
Employees must comply with standards applicable to their actions

1926.21.title: Safety Training and Education
requirements: Employer must instruct each employee in recognition and avoidance of unsafe conditions
Training in regulations applicable to work environment
Employees working in confined or enclosed spaces must be instructed on hazards

key_definitions.competent_person: One who is capable of identifying existing and predictable hazards in the surroundings or working conditions which are unsanitary, hazardous, or dangerous to employees, and who has authorization to take prompt corrective measures to eliminate them.
key_definitions.qualified_person: One who, by possession of a recognized degree, certificate, or professional standing, or who by extensive knowledge, training, and experience, has successfully demonstrated the ability to solve or resolve problems relating to the subject matter, the work, or the project.

1926.34.title: Means of Egress
requirements: Every building or structure must have sufficient exits for prompt escape
Exit routes must be maintained and free of obstruction
Exits must be marked with illuminated exit signs

1926.50.title: Medical Services and First Aid
requirements: First aid supplies must be available at the jobsite
In the absence of an infirmary, clinic, or hospital near the worksite, a person trained in first aid must be available
First aid kits must be checked weekly to ensure completeness
Emergency phone numbers must be posted
Where 911 is not available, telephone or radio communication to emergency services required

thresholds.potable_water: Must be provided at all job sites
thresholds.toilets: 1 per 20 workers (minimum), 1 per 40 workers if serviced regularly

thresholds.action_level: 85 dBA TWA (8-hour) — hearing conservation program required
thresholds.PEL: 90 dBA TWA (8-hour) — engineering/administrative controls or hearing protection required

1926.55.title: Gases, Vapors, Fumes, Dusts, and Mists

training_levels.awareness: For workers near hazwaste operations but not handling
training_levels.operations_24hr: Workers performing cleanup with limited exposure

1926.95.title: Criteria for PPE
requirements: Employer must assess workplace for hazards requiring PPE
Written hazard assessment certification required
PPE must properly fit each affected employee
Employer must provide PPE at no cost to employee
Training on when PPE is necessary, what type, proper use, limitations, care, and disposal

1926.100.title: Head Protection

types.type_I: Protects top of head from impact
types.type_II: Protects top and sides from impact

1926.100.replacement: Replace after any impact, after 5 years from manufacture date (or per manufacturer), immediately if cracked/dented/damaged

1926.101.title: Hearing Protection
1926.101.requirements: Required when exposures exceed PEL (90 dBA) and engineering/admin controls not feasible. Double protection (plugs + muffs) recommended above 105 dBA.

requirements.safety_glasses: Required at all times on industrial construction sites
requirements.face_shield: Grinding, cutting, chemical splash (worn over safety glasses)
requirements.welding_hood: Required for arc welding, minimum shade based on amperage

1926.103.title: Respiratory Protection
requirements: Written respiratory protection program required
Medical evaluation before fit testing
Annual fit testing for tight-fitting respirators
Clean shaven where respirator seals to face
Assigned Protection Factors (APF): half-mask = 10, full-face = 50, PAPR = 25-1000, SCBA = 10,000

1926.104.title: Safety Belts, Lifelines, and Lanyards
1926.104.note: Largely superseded by Subpart M (1926.500+) for fall protection

1926.107.title: Definitions (PPE subpart)
1926.107.fr_clothing_note: FR clothing required per NFPA 2112/NFPA 70E or site-specific requirements. Most refinery/industrial sites require minimum FR clothing as standard PPE.

typical_refinery_ppe_minimums: Hard hat (Type I, Class E preferred)
Steel-toe or composite-toe boots (ASTM F2413, minimum 6-inch)
FR clothing (NFPA 2112 rated, minimum ATPV 8 cal/cm2)
Hearing protection (available, worn in posted areas)
H2S monitor / 4-gas monitor in process areas
High-visibility vest or FR hi-vis shirt

1926.404.title: Wiring Design and Protection
1926.404.gfci_requirement: GFCI protection required on all 120V, single-phase, 15 and 20-amp receptacle outlets on construction sites that are not part of permanent wiring. Alternative: assured equipment grounding conductor program.
1926.404.grounding: All electrical equipment must be grounded or double-insulated

1926.405.title: Wiring Methods
key_points: Temporary wiring must be removed after construction complete
Flexible cords used only for pendants, fixtures, appliances, or temporary purposes
NM cable (Romex) not permitted for temporary wiring on construction sites

1926.416.title: Safety-Related Work Practices
requirements: No work near energized parts unless deenergized or properly guarded
Minimum approach distances must be maintained
Circuits must be deenergized before work unless deenergizing creates greater hazard

1926.417.title: Lockout/Tagout
requirements: Controls must be deactivated and locked/tagged in the off position
Each employee must apply their own lock/tag
Locks/tags must not be removed by anyone other than the person who applied them
Stored energy must be dissipated or restrained
loto_steps: 3. Shut down equipment using normal procedures
7. Verify zero energy state (try to start equipment)

1926.431_to_432.title: Maintenance and Environmental Deterioration
1926.431_to_432.requirement: Electrical equipment must be maintained in safe condition, free from hazards like broken insulation, exposed conductors

arc_flash_nfpa_70E.standard: NFPA 70E Standard for Electrical Safety in the Workplace
arc_flash_nfpa_70E.note: Not directly an OSHA standard but enforced under General Duty Clause

category_1.ppe: Arc-rated shirt/pants (min 4 cal), face shield, hard hat, safety glasses, hearing protection, leather gloves

category_2.ppe: Arc-rated shirt/pants (min 8 cal), arc-rated flash hood or face shield + balaclava, hard hat, safety glasses, hearing protection, leather gloves

category_3.ppe: Arc-rated flash suit (min 25 cal), arc-rated flash hood, hard hat, safety glasses, hearing protection, rubber insulating gloves + leather protectors

category_4.ppe: Arc-rated flash suit (min 40 cal), arc-rated flash suit hood, hard hat, safety glasses, hearing protection, rubber insulating gloves + leather protectors

arc_flash_nfpa_70E.max_working_limit: 40 cal/cm2 — above this, work is not permitted

key_thresholds.platform_width: Minimum 18 inches wide (20 inches for plastering/stucco)
key_thresholds.midrail_height: Approximately halfway between platform and top rail
key_thresholds.toeboard: Minimum 3.5 inches tall, within 1/4 inch of platform
key_thresholds.plank_overhang: Minimum 6 inches, maximum 12 inches beyond support (unless cleated)
key_thresholds.fall_protection_trigger: 10 feet above lower level for scaffolds
key_thresholds.maximum_plank_gap: 1 inch between planks (exception: when wider gap necessary for specific work)
key_thresholds.load_rating: Must support 4x maximum intended load without failure
key_thresholds.access: Must provide safe access when platform is more than 2 feet above or below point of access (ladder, stair tower, ramp, or equivalent)

1926.452.title: Additional Requirements for Specific Types
supported_scaffold_notes: Mudsills or adequate firm foundation required
Plumb and level — adjust only with screw jacks, not unstable objects
Cross-bracing not permitted as means of access
Scaffold must be secured to structure when height-to-base-width ratio exceeds 4:1

1926.453.title: Aerial Lifts
requirements: Only trained/authorized operators
Body harness and lanyard required in boom-supported lifts (attached to boom or basket, never to adjacent structure)
Not required in scissor lifts per OSHA (some sites still require it)
Set brakes when on incline
Do not exceed rated capacity
Do not belt off to an adjacent structure from an aerial lift

1926.454.title: Training Requirements for Scaffolds
requirements: Each employee who works on a scaffold must be trained by a qualified person
Training covers: hazards, proper use, load capacities, fall protection
Competent person must train erectors and dismantlers on specific scaffold type
Retraining required when: hazards change, scaffold type changes, deficiencies observed, or knowledge is inadequate

competent_person_duties: Inspect scaffold before each shift and after any event that could affect structural integrity
Tag scaffold as safe (green tag) or unsafe (red tag)
Authority to remove workers from scaffold immediately
Determine if conditions are safe to erect, use, dismantle

scaffold_tag_system.green_tag: Safe to use — inspected by competent person, meets all requirements
scaffold_tag_system.yellow_tag: Caution — specific restrictions apply (noted on tag). May have limitations like 'no more than 2 workers' or 'light duty only'
scaffold_tag_system.red_tag: Danger — do NOT use. Under construction, dismantling, damaged, or failed inspection. Only authorized erectors may access.

1926.501.title: Duty to Have Fall Protection
1926.501.general_trigger: 6 feet above a lower level for construction (general industry is 4 feet)

specific_triggers.unprotected_sides_edges: 6 feet — guardrail, safety net, or personal fall arrest
specific_triggers.leading_edges: 6 feet — same protections or fall restraint
specific_triggers.hoist_areas: 6 feet — guardrail or personal fall arrest
specific_triggers.holes: 6 feet for walking/working surfaces with holes — covers or guardrails
specific_triggers.excavations: 6 feet — guardrails, fences, or barricades at edges
specific_triggers.dangerous_equipment: Less than 6 feet if fall would be into dangerous equipment — guardrails or equipment guards
specific_triggers.roofing_low_slope: 6 feet — guardrail, safety net, or personal fall arrest
specific_triggers.roofing_steep_slope: 6 feet — guardrail, safety net, or personal fall arrest
specific_triggers.steel_erection: 15 feet for connectors, 30 feet for decking (per Subpart R)

1926.502.title: Fall Protection Systems Criteria and Practices

guardrail_specs.midrail: Halfway between top edge of guardrail and working level
guardrail_specs.top_rail_strength: 200 lbs applied in any outward or downward direction
guardrail_specs.midrail_strength: 150 lbs in any downward or outward direction

safety_net_specs.installation: No more than 30 feet below working surface
safety_net_specs.horizontal_extension: 8 feet for up to 5-foot fall distance, 10 feet for 5-10 feet, 13 feet for more than 10 feet
safety_net_specs.drop_test: 400 lb bag of sand, 28-32 inches diameter, dropped from highest walking/working surface

personal_fall_arrest.components: Full body harness (body belts prohibited for fall arrest), lanyard or retractable, anchor point
personal_fall_arrest.anchor_point_strength: 5,000 lbs per person attached, or designed by qualified person with 2:1 safety factor
personal_fall_arrest.total_fall_distance: Must not contact lower level — calculate: free fall + deceleration + harness elongation + D-ring shift + safety margin
personal_fall_arrest.harness_inspection: Before each use — check webbing, stitching, D-rings, buckles, labels
personal_fall_arrest.after_fall: Remove from service after any fall arrest event — inspect by competent person before reuse (most manufacturers require retirement)

warning_line_systems.distance_from_edge: 6 feet from edge on low-slope roofs

1926.503.title: Training Requirements
requirements: Trained by competent person
Must recognize fall hazards and procedures to minimize them
Training on proper use, operation, and limitations of fall protection systems
Written certification of training required (name, date, signature of trainer)
Retraining when: inadequate knowledge, changes in equipment or procedures, workplace changes

fall_protection_plan_option.when_used: Only when conventional fall protection (guardrail, safety net, personal fall arrest) is infeasible or creates greater hazard
requirements: Developed by qualified person
Site-specific, not generic
Identifies why conventional methods infeasible
Describes alternative measures
Must be part of written safety and health program

common_fall_protection_for_construction.personal_fall_arrest_system: Harness + shock-absorbing lanyard or retractable lifeline + rated anchor
common_fall_protection_for_construction.fall_restraint: System that prevents worker from reaching the fall hazard — shorter than personal fall arrest distance
common_fall_protection_for_construction.self_retracting_lifeline: Limits free fall to approximately 2 feet, preferred over standard lanyards
common_fall_protection_for_construction.horizontal_lifeline: Must be designed by qualified person — each worker adds load to system
common_fall_protection_for_construction.vertical_lifeline: One worker per lifeline — rope grab device

key_definitions.excavation: Any man-made cut, cavity, trench, or depression in an earth surface formed by earth removal
key_definitions.trench: A narrow excavation (depth greater than width, width not exceeding 15 feet)
key_definitions.competent_person: Must be capable of identifying existing and predictable hazards and authorized to take corrective measures

requirements.utility_location: Estimated location of underground utilities must be determined before excavation. Contact 811 (Call Before You Dig) at least 48-72 hours prior. Hand dig within 18-24 inches of known utilities.
requirements.access_egress: Ladder, steps, or ramp required when excavation is 4 feet or deeper. Must be within 25 feet of lateral travel for workers.
requirements.exposure_to_vehicle_traffic: Workers must be provided with and wear high-visibility vests
requirements.falling_loads: No workers under loads handled by lifting/digging equipment. Stand away from vehicle being loaded/unloaded.
requirements.water_accumulation: Must have adequate precautions — pumping, special support, water diversion. Do not work in accumulated water without adequate protection.
requirements.stability_of_adjacent_structures: Underpinning or support may be required. Must not excavate below base of foundation without support system.
requirements.fall_protection_at_edge: Guardrails, fences, or barricades when excavation is not readily seen (6 feet per Subpart M if walking/working surface)
requirements.inspections: Daily before each shift, after rainstorm, and after any event that could change conditions. Competent person must inspect.

1926.652.title: Requirements for Protective Systems
1926.652.trigger_depth: 5 feet — protective systems required for excavations 5 feet or deeper (unless excavation is in stable rock). Exemption: competent person determines no indication of potential cave-in in excavations less than 5 feet.
1926.652.20_foot_rule: Excavations 20 feet or deeper must be designed by a registered professional engineer

soil_classification.stable_rock: Natural solid mineral material that can be excavated with vertical sides and remain intact while exposed
soil_classification.type_A: Most stable soil — cohesive, unconfined compressive strength 1.5 TSF or greater (clay, silty clay, hardpan). Cannot be previously disturbed, subject to vibration, fissured, or have seeping water.
soil_classification.type_B: Medium stability — cohesive with compressive strength 0.5-1.5 TSF (angular gravel, silt, silty clay, previously disturbed Type A soils, dry rock not stable)
soil_classification.type_C: Least stable — cohesive with compressive strength 0.5 TSF or less (gravel, sand, loamy sand, submerged soil, soil from which water is seeping, submerged rock not stable)

maximum_allowable_slopes.type_A: 3/4H:1V (53 degrees) — short term (24hr max) 1/2H:1V (63 degrees) for depths under 12 feet

protective_systems.sloping: Cut back trench wall at angle. Simplest method.
protective_systems.benching: Cut sides into steps. Only for Type A and B soils. NOT allowed in Type C.
protective_systems.shoring: Install supports (hydraulic, pneumatic, or timber) to prevent cave-in. Must be installed top-down, removed bottom-up.
protective_systems.shielding: Trench boxes/shields to protect workers. Does not prevent cave-in but protects workers inside shield.
protective_systems.tabulated_data: Use manufacturer data sheets for shoring/shielding systems

competent_person_excavation_duties: Inspect excavation daily, before each shift, after rain, and after any hazard-increasing occurrence
Design protective system or use tabulated data
Remove workers from excavation when hazardous conditions exist
Ensure utilities are located and protected
Ensure access/egress available within 25 feet lateral travel

visual: Observe soil as it is excavated
Look for layering, fissures, seeping water
Check spoil pile for clumping (cohesive) vs. running (granular)

manual.thumb_penetration: Press thumb into soil clump — Type A: indent with difficulty; Type B: indent easily; Type C: thumb penetrates full depth easily
manual.ribbon_test: Roll soil into thread and let hang — Type A: 2+ inch ribbon without breaking; Type C: cannot form ribbon
manual.pocket_penetrometer: Reads directly in TSF/tons per square foot

1926.752.title: Site Layout and Construction Sequence
requirements: Site-specific erection plan developed by qualified person for multi-story structures
Pre-planning meeting required between controlling contractor and steel erector
Adequate access roads and grading for crane/equipment setup

1926.753.title: Hoisting and Rigging
requirements: All hoisting equipment inspected before each shift
Tag lines used to control loads
Multiple lift rigging assemblies: maximum 5 members per assembly

1926.756.title: Beams and Columns
requirements: Minimum of 2 bolts per connection before load released
Columns must be anchored by minimum of 4 anchor bolts
Column splices evaluated by qualified person

thresholds.general: 15 feet for connectors (higher than standard 6-foot trigger)
thresholds.connectors: Protected above 15 feet, or above 2 stories/30 feet (whichever is less)
thresholds.deckers: Protected above 15 feet, or above 2 stories/30 feet

controlled_decking_zone.description: Designated area for initial installation of metal decking where conventional fall protection may not be used
requirements: Width of CDZ: 90 feet or width of bay, whichever is less
Only workers engaged in decking allowed in CDZ
Perimeter controlled by control lines or equivalent
Unsecured decking limited to 3,000 sq ft

1926.1402.title: Ground Conditions
requirements: Controlling entity must ensure ground conditions are adequate
Crane operator informed of ground conditions including underground hazards
Adequate drainage, firm footing, level ground for cranes

1926.1404.title: Assembly/Disassembly — General
requirements: Must be directed by qualified or competent person (A/D director)
A/D must be done under direct supervision

1926.1408_1411.title: Power Line Safety

clearances.unknown_voltage: Assume 50kV — maintain 10 feet minimum. Contact utility to determine voltage.

1926.1408_1411.assembly_disassembly: Minimum clearance is 10 feet from lines up to 350kV unless utility confirms de-energized/grounded

frequencies.shift_inspection: Before each shift by operator — functional check of controls, safety devices, operational aids
frequencies.monthly: Items specified in manufacturer procedures — documented
frequencies.annual: Comprehensive inspection by competent person — documented with date, signature, serial number
frequencies.post_assembly: After each assembly — check connections, pins, bolts, structural members

1926.1417.title: Operation
critical_rules: Tag lines required to control loads
Operator must not leave controls with load suspended
Load chart must be in cab and legible

1926.1425.title: Keeping Clear of the Load
rules: No one may be under a suspended load
No one may stand or walk under boom of any crane

1926.1427.title: Operator Qualification and Certification
requirements: Operators must be certified by accredited testing organization (NCCCO, CIC, NCCER, or OECP)
Certification must match crane type and capacity
Employer must ensure operator is qualified for specific equipment
Medical fitness: eyesight able to recognize signals at distance, able to determine load position, no conditions preventing safe operation

1926.1431.title: Hoisting Personnel
requirements: Personnel platforms only when conventional means (ladder, stairway, etc.) are more hazardous or not possible
Platform must be designed by qualified person — minimum 2:1 safety factor
Platform load limited to 50% of rated crane capacity for that radius
Pre-lift meeting required
Trial lift at each new location required
Continuous communication between operator, signal person, and personnel in platform
Must be qualified by third-party evaluation or employer evaluation
Must know and understand standard hand signals (ASME B30.5)
Signal person must be in clear view of operator or use radio
When operating near power lines, a dedicated spotter may be required
Qualified rigger required for assembly/disassembly
Qualified rigger for hooking, unhooking, and guiding loads near workers

critical_lift_criteria.note: Not an OSHA-defined term but widely used in industrial construction
typical_criteria: Load exceeds 75% of crane rated capacity for configuration
Lift of a load whose weight is not accurately known
Loads requiring exceptional care (long/flexible, high-value, radioactive)
critical_lift_plan_elements: Load weight verified by calculation or certified scales
Crane configuration (boom length, radius, capacity at radius)
Rigging plan with rated capacity of all components

requirements.fire_extinguisher_placement: Within 100 feet travel distance (general). Within 50 feet of more than 5 gallons of flammable/combustible liquids or 5 lbs of flammable gas.
requirements.extinguisher_rating: Minimum 2A rating. Where flammable liquids present, minimum 20-B:C
requirements.inspection: Monthly inspection. Annual servicing/maintenance. 6-year maintenance for stored pressure. 12-year hydrostatic test.
requirements.fire_alarm: Must be provided when warranted by construction project
requirements.water_supply: Minimum 100 GPM supply for first floor, 500 gallons/minute for multi-story

1926.152.title: Flammable Liquids

storage_limits.inside_building: 25 gallons outside of approved storage cabinet
storage_limits.approved_cabinet: 60 gallons of Class I/II, or 120 gallons of Class III per cabinet. Maximum 3 cabinets per area.
storage_limits.outside_storage: Must be at least 20 feet from any building

1926.152.dispensing: Only approved containers with flash-arresting screens. Containers over 1 gallon must be safety cans.

flash_point_classes.class_IA: Flash point below 73F, boiling point below 100F (e.g., pentane)
flash_point_classes.class_IB: Flash point below 73F, boiling point at or above 100F (e.g., gasoline, acetone)
flash_point_classes.class_IC: Flash point at or above 73F but below 100F (e.g., xylene)
flash_point_classes.class_II: Flash point at or above 100F but below 140F (e.g., diesel, kerosene)
flash_point_classes.class_IIIA: Flash point at or above 140F but below 200F (e.g., mineral oil)
flash_point_classes.class_IIIB: Flash point at or above 200F (e.g., cooking oils)

section_02_permit_systems.title: Permit Systems for Industrial/Refinery Construction
general_principles: Permits ensure work is reviewed and authorized before starting
Permits identify hazards and required precautions
Permits create accountability — specific individuals authorize specific work
Permits must be available at the work location during work
When conditions change, work stops and permit is re-evaluated
Expired permits must be closed and new permits issued

hot_work_permit.description: Required for any work producing sparks, flame, or heat — welding, cutting, grinding, brazing, soldering, heat guns, open flame
hot_work_permit.osha_reference: 29 CFR 1926.352 (welding), NFPA 51B (Standard for Fire Prevention During Welding, Cutting, and Other Hot Work)
hot_work_permit.typical_duration: One shift (8-12 hours). New permit required each shift.
hot_work_permit.who_issues: Area authority (client/owner representative), or designated safety representative

who_signs.issuer: Client/owner area authority or designated representative
who_signs.performer: Craft supervisor or foreman of the crew performing hot work
who_signs.fire_watch: Designated fire watch person (name on permit)

requirements_before_issuing: Area inspected for combustibles within 35 feet
Combustibles removed or covered with fire-resistant blankets/shields
Floor/wall openings within 35 feet covered or guarded
Atmospheric testing if near process equipment — LEL must be below 10% of LEL (most sites use 0% LEL as threshold)
Fire extinguisher (minimum 20-B:C) within 50 feet
Fire watch designated (must remain 30 minutes after hot work ceases)
Verify no flammable liquids/gases in piping being cut/welded (line cleared, blinded, or verified zero energy)
Sprinkler systems not impaired (or compensating measures in place)
Ventilation adequate
fire_watch_requirements: Dedicated role — no other tasks during fire watch
Maintain watch during hot work and minimum 30 minutes after (some sites require 60 minutes)
Must have unobstructed view of hot work area
Fire extinguisher and communication device readily available
Authority to stop hot work if conditions change
Know how to activate fire alarm
hot_work_permit.renewal: New permit each shift. Re-evaluation if conditions change, work area changes, or scope changes.

confined_space_entry_permit.description: Required for entry into any permit-required confined space — tanks, vessels, manholes, pits, vaults, silos, pipelines (large diameter)
confined_space_entry_permit.typical_duration: One shift (8-12 hours). Some sites limit to specific time windows.
confined_space_entry_permit.who_issues: Entry supervisor (competent person authorized by employer)

who_signs.entry_supervisor: Verifies conditions are safe, authorizes entry, must be on-site or immediately available
who_signs.entrants: Each person entering signs acknowledging hazards and procedures
who_signs.attendant: Person stationed outside space — signs acknowledging duties

requirements_before_issuing: Space identified as permit-required confined space (PRCS) in written program
Hazards of the space identified
Atmospheric testing completed — O2, LEL, H2S, CO at minimum
Atmospheric testing from outside before entry, then continuous monitoring during entry
Space ventilated (continuous mechanical ventilation recommended)
Energy isolation (LOTO) of all energy sources to the space
Lines broken, blanked, or blinded to prevent introduction of hazardous materials
Rescue plan and equipment in place (on-site rescue team or arranged external rescue within response time)
Communication between entrant and attendant established
Entry/exit equipment in place (ladders, harness/retrieval system)
PPE determined and available

CO.maximum: 25 ppm (many sites use 25 ppm as action level)

attendant_duties: Remain at entry point at all times while entrants are inside
Monitor atmospheric conditions (remote display or communication with entrants)
Maintain accurate count of entrants (entry/exit log)
Communicate with entrants to monitor their status
Order evacuation if: hazardous condition detected, behavioral effects observed, situation outside scope of permit, attendant cannot perform duties
Do NOT enter the space for rescue (unless trained and relieved by another attendant)
Keep unauthorized persons out of the space
cancellation_triggers: Conditions change from those specified on permit
Any entrant reports symptoms (dizziness, nausea, irritation)

energized_electrical_work_permit.description: Required when electrical work must be performed on energized equipment (live work). Most sites require de-energization as first option.
energized_electrical_work_permit.osha_reference: 29 CFR 1926.416-417 (Construction), NFPA 70E Article 110.3 (Energized Electrical Work Permit)
energized_electrical_work_permit.justification_required: Must document why de-energization creates greater hazard or is infeasible (e.g., hospital life-safety, continuous process where shutdown creates greater risk, testing/troubleshooting that requires energized state)
energized_electrical_work_permit.typical_duration: Specific to the task. Expires when task is complete or conditions change.

who_signs.qualified_worker: Person performing the work — must be qualified per NFPA 70E

requirements: Arc flash analysis completed for equipment
Shock and arc flash hazard assessment documented
Appropriate PPE category determined and PPE available
Shock approach boundaries identified and controlled
Arc flash boundary identified and controlled
Insulated tools rated for voltage
Rubber insulating gloves (tested and within certification)
Job briefing completed with all affected workers
Emergency procedures reviewed (CPR/AED availability)
Rescue plan for electrical shock/arc flash

excavation_permit.description: Required before any ground disturbance — digging, trenching, boring, potholing, grading that could encounter underground utilities or create cave-in hazard
excavation_permit.typical_duration: Duration of excavation activity, typically renewed weekly or when scope changes

who_signs.competent_person: Person trained in soil classification and protective systems
who_signs.utility_locator: Confirmation that 811 locate has been completed

requirements_before_issuing: 811 utility locate completed (minimum 48-72 hours advance notice)
Site-specific utility drawings reviewed
Competent person assigned for duration of work
Protective system determined (slope, shore, shield)
Access/egress plan (ladders within 25 feet lateral travel for 4+ foot depth)
Spoil pile placement determined (minimum 2 feet from edge of excavation)
Traffic control plan if near roads/travel ways
Nearby structures evaluated for stability
Water control plan if water table or drainage is a concern
PPE requirements identified (high-vis vest minimum)

line_break_permit.description: Required before opening any process piping, tubing, or equipment that has contained or could contain hazardous substances. Common in refinery/petrochemical construction.
line_break_permit.typical_duration: Specific to the task, usually one shift

who_signs.operations_representative: Confirms line is isolated, depressurized, drained, and safe
who_signs.safety_representative: Reviews atmospheric monitoring and PPE requirements

requirements: Line identified and verified (P&IDs reviewed, line walked)
Line contents identified (SDS reviewed for all potential contents)
Line isolated (block valves closed, blinds installed, or disconnected)
Line depressurized and drained
Atmospheric testing at break point
Appropriate PPE (splash protection, respiratory protection based on contents)
Containment for residual materials (drip pans, absorbent, containment)
Emergency equipment available (eyewash within 10 seconds travel if corrosives)
All workers briefed on hazards
Fire watch if flammable contents possible

radiography_permit.description: Required when radiographic testing (X-ray or gamma ray) is performed. Creates radiation exclusion zones.
radiography_permit.regulatory_reference: 10 CFR 34 (NRC), state radiation control programs, OSHA 29 CFR 1926.53 (ionizing radiation)
radiography_permit.typical_duration: Duration of radiographic activities (often performed on night shift to minimize personnel exposure)

who_signs.radiographer: Licensed radiographer (must have specific state/NRC license)
who_signs.area_authority: Confirms area cleared of non-essential personnel

requirements: Radiographer has valid license and badge (dosimeter)
Exclusion zone established (roped off, posted with radiation warning signs)
All personnel evacuated from exclusion zone
Site-wide notification (PA system, radio, or direct notification)
Survey meters used to verify boundary radiation levels
Time, distance, shielding principles applied
Emergency procedures for source stuck in exposed position
Exposures logged per NRC/state requirements

scaffold_permit_tag.description: Authorization and inspection system for scaffold use. While not a 'permit' per OSHA, most industrial sites use a formal scaffold tag and inspection system.
typical_process: Scaffold erected by trained/qualified erectors per design or manufacturer requirements
Competent person inspects scaffold upon completion
Tag attached: green (safe), yellow (restricted), or red (do not use)
Competent person inspects before each shift
tag_must_include: Name of competent person who inspected

crane_lift_plan.description: Required documentation for crane lifts, especially critical lifts. Most industrial sites require a lift plan for every crane operation.

standard_lift_plan: Load description and weight (verified, not estimated)
Rigging plan (slings, shackles, rated capacity of each)
Wind speed limitations (typically stop at 20-25 mph sustained)
critical_lift_additional: Engineered lift plan by qualified person
Load weight verified by certified scales, certified drawings, or calculation
All rigging components load-tested or certified
Detailed setup diagram showing positions, radii, elevations
Pre-lift meeting with all involved personnel

work_at_height_permit.description: Many industrial sites require a separate work at height permit in addition to standard fall protection requirements. Documents hazards, controls, and rescue plan.
typical_requirements: Work method specified (scaffold, aerial lift, ladder, rope access)
Rescue plan documented (self-rescue, assisted rescue, technical rescue)
Exclusion zones for dropped object potential

first_aid.definition: Treatment that does not meet the criteria for a recordable case
examples: Cleaning, flushing, or soaking surface wounds
Non-prescription medications at nonprescription strength (first use only)
Drilling of fingernail/toenail to relieve pressure
Removal of foreign bodies from eye using irrigation or cotton swab
Removal of splinters or foreign material from areas other than the eye by irrigation, tweezers, cotton swabs, or other simple means
Use of temporary immobilization devices while transporting

recordable.definition: Work-related injury or illness that results in any of the following
criteria: Restricted work or transfer to another job
Significant injury or illness diagnosed by physician (cancer, fracture, punctured eardrum, chronic irreversible disease)
medical_treatment_beyond_first_aid_inclu: Prescription medications (second use of non-prescription counts as medical treatment)
Sutures/stitches (wound closure devices like Steri-strips are first aid)
Physical therapy or chiropractic treatment after first visit
Removing foreign bodies from the eye if embedded or if medical professional needed

restricted_work_case.definition: Employee cannot perform one or more routine functions of their job, or cannot work a full shift
restricted_work_case.counting: Count calendar days from day after injury, cap at 180 days
restricted_work_case.routine_functions: Work activities the employee regularly performs at least once per week

lost_time_case.definition: Days away from work — employee cannot report to work due to work-related injury/illness
lost_time_case.counting: Count calendar days starting the day after injury. Do not count day of injury. Maximum 180 days. Include weekends/holidays if employee would not have been able to work.
lost_time_case.return_to_work: Employer may offer restricted/light duty to avoid lost time classification

fatality.reporting_requirement: Must report to OSHA within 8 hours
fatality.method: Call OSHA Area Office, call OSHA 24-hour hotline (1-800-321-OSHA), or report online
fatality.time_limit: Death must occur within 30 days of the work-related incident

hospitalization_amputation_eye_loss.reporting_requirement: Must report to OSHA within 24 hours
hospitalization_amputation_eye_loss.criteria: In-patient hospitalization (not ER visit), amputation (including fingertip with bone loss), loss of an eye
hospitalization_amputation_eye_loss.time_limit: Must occur within 24 hours of work-related incident

five_whys.description: Iterative interrogative technique to explore cause-and-effect relationships
process: 1. State the problem clearly
2. Ask 'Why did this happen?' — document the answer
3. For each answer, ask 'Why?' again
4. Repeat until root cause is identified (typically 5 iterations, may be more or fewer)
5. Root cause should be something that can be corrected with a systemic change
tips: May branch — one problem can have multiple causes
Stop when you reach a cause that can be controlled/changed
Avoid blaming individuals — look for system failures
Verify each 'why' with evidence, not assumptions

example.why_1: Worker leaned over guardrail to reach work — guardrail was not at proper height
example.why_2: Scaffold was modified after initial inspection — cross brace removed to access equipment
example.why_3: No re-inspection after modification — competent person was not notified
example.why_4: No formal process to trigger re-inspection after scaffold modifications
example.why_5: Scaffold management program did not include modification notification requirements
example.root_cause: Scaffold management program deficiency — no procedure for post-modification re-inspection

fishbone_diagram.description: Visual tool that categorizes potential causes of a problem to identify root cause
categories_for_safety: Equipment/Tools (defective, wrong for job, not inspected, not maintained)
Environment (weather, lighting, noise, congestion, confined space)
Materials (SDS not available, wrong material, deteriorated)
process: 1. Define the problem (effect) — place at the head of the fish
2. Draw main bones for each category
3. Brainstorm causes under each category
4. Ask 'Why?' for each cause to find deeper causes (sub-bones)
5. Identify most likely root cause(s) based on evidence
6. Verify root cause with data/evidence

taproot.description: Systematic root cause analysis process using a structured investigation tree
phases: 1. Gather information (SnapCharT — timeline of events, conditions, and actions)
3. Analyze each causal factor using Root Cause Tree
4. Develop corrective actions using Corrective Action Helper
5. Track and verify corrective action effectiveness
root_cause_categories: Procedures (not used, followed incorrectly, wrong, incomplete)
Training (no training, incomplete, understanding not verified)
Management system (SPAC — Standards, Policies, or Administrative Controls)
Human engineering (human factors — labels, displays, workplace layout)

near_miss_reporting.definition: An event that did not result in injury, illness, or damage but had the potential to do so
importance: Heinrich's Triangle: For every 1 serious injury, there are approximately 10 minor injuries, 30 property damage incidents, and 600 near-misses
Near-miss reporting identifies hazards before they cause harm
Leading indicator of safety program effectiveness
effective_program_elements: Simple reporting mechanism (card, app, QR code)
Feedback loop — reporter notified of actions taken
Trending and analysis of near-miss data
Target: Most effective sites aim for 10:1 near-miss to incident ratio or higher

immediate_0_to_1_hour: Ensure scene is safe and provide medical care
Secure the incident scene (do not disturb unless necessary for rescue)
Begin collecting perishable evidence (photos, atmospheric readings, witness locations)
short_term_1_to_24_hours: Report to OSHA if required (8 hours for fatality, 24 hours for hospitalization/amputation/eye loss)
Conduct initial witness interviews (separate, not group)
Document scene with photos, measurements, sketches
Collect physical evidence (broken equipment, PPE, tools)
Review relevant documents (permits, JSAs, training records, inspection logs)
investigation_1_to_5_days: Identify contributing factors and root cause(s)
follow_up_5_to_30_days: Communicate findings and lessons learned (toolbox talks, safety alerts)
Update JSAs, procedures, training as needed
Close out investigation with management review

elements: Unique tracking number for each corrective action
Status tracking (open, in progress, completed, verified)
hierarchy_of_corrective_actions: Elimination — remove the hazard entirely (most effective)
Substitution — replace with less hazardous material/process
Engineering controls — isolate people from hazard (guards, ventilation, barriers)
Administrative controls — change the way people work (procedures, training, scheduling)
PPE — protect the worker (least effective, last resort)

TRIR.formula: (Number of OSHA Recordable Cases x 200,000) / Total Hours Worked
TRIR.200000_factor: Represents 100 full-time workers working 40 hours/week for 50 weeks
TRIR.example: 5 recordable cases with 1,000,000 hours worked = (5 x 200,000) / 1,000,000 = 1.0
notes: Includes all OSHA recordable cases: death, days away, restricted work, medical treatment beyond first aid
Does not include first aid cases
Many owner/operators set TRIR thresholds for contractor pre-qualification (commonly require below 1.0)

DART_rate.full_name: Days Away, Restricted, or Transferred Rate
DART_rate.formula: (Number of DART Cases x 200,000) / Total Hours Worked
DART_rate.what_counts: Only recordable cases that result in days away from work, restricted work activity, or job transfer
DART_rate.example: 3 DART cases with 1,000,000 hours worked = (3 x 200,000) / 1,000,000 = 0.6
DART_rate.notes: DART rate is always equal to or lower than TRIR (DART cases are a subset of all recordable cases)

LTIR.formula: (Number of Lost Time Cases x 200,000) / Total Hours Worked
LTIR.what_counts: Only recordable cases that result in one or more days away from work (does not include restricted duty or transfer)
LTIR.notes: LTIR is always equal to or lower than DART rate

severity_rate.formula: (Total Days Away/Restricted x 200,000) / Total Hours Worked
severity_rate.purpose: Measures severity of injuries, not just frequency

EMR.description: Workers compensation insurance modifier based on company's claims history vs. industry average
EMR.calculation: Calculated by NCCI (National Council on Compensation Insurance) or state equivalent. Based on 3 prior policy years, excluding most recent year.
EMR.impact: Directly affects workers comp premiums. Also used as contractor pre-qualification criterion. Many owners require EMR below 1.0 (some require below 0.80).
EMR.note: EMR is retrospective — it reflects past performance and takes time to improve. A single fatality or severe injury can affect EMR for 3+ years.

fatality_rate.formula: (Number of Fatalities x 200,000) / Total Hours Worked

leading_indicators.description: Proactive measures that predict future safety performance. More valuable than lagging indicators because they identify problems before injuries occur.

safety_observations.target: Varies by site — common targets: 1 observation per supervisor per day, or 1 per 1,000 hours worked
safety_observations.safe_to_at_risk_ratio: Track percentage of safe vs. at-risk observations. Improving ratio indicates improving safety culture.

near_miss_reports.target: Should significantly exceed incident count. Target ratio of 10:1 or higher (near-misses to recordables).

jsa_jha_completion.description: Percentage of tasks with completed JSA/JHA before work begins
jsa_jha_completion.target: 100% — every task must have a JSA reviewed before starting

training_completion.description: Percentage of required training completed on time
training_completion.target: 100% — all workers current on all required training

corrective_action_closure_rate.description: Percentage of corrective actions closed within target timeframe

safety_meeting_attendance.description: Percentage of workers attending required safety meetings (toolbox talks, orientations)

stop_work_authority_use.description: Number of SWA exercises — higher is generally better (shows engaged workforce)
stop_work_authority_use.target: Track trending — should not be zero

audit_scores.description: Scores from internal and external safety audits

permit_compliance.description: Percentage of permits correctly completed and in place before work begins

inspection_completion.description: Percentage of required inspections completed on schedule (equipment, scaffolds, excavations, fire extinguishers)

section_05_jsa_jha.title: Job Safety Analysis / Job Hazard Analysis
section_05_jsa_jha.definition: A technique that focuses on job tasks as a way to identify hazards before they occur. Involves breaking a job into steps, identifying hazards at each step, and determining preventive measures.
creation_process: 1. Select the job to analyze (prioritize: new, changed, high-hazard, or those with incident history)
2. Break the job into sequential steps (typically 8-15 steps, no more than 20)
3. Identify hazards associated with each step
4. Determine preventive measures for each hazard using hierarchy of controls
5. Review with workers who perform the job
6. Communicate to all workers before starting the job
7. Review and update when conditions, equipment, or personnel change

hazard_categories.struck_by: Workers hit by moving objects, vehicles, falling materials, swinging loads
hazard_categories.struck_against: Workers striking against stationary objects, sharp edges, protruding materials
hazard_categories.caught_in_between: Body parts caught in machinery, pinch points, collapsing materials, cave-ins
hazard_categories.falls: Falls from elevation (ladders, scaffolds, roofs, openings) or same level (slips, trips)
hazard_categories.overexertion: Lifting, pushing, pulling, repetitive motion, awkward postures
hazard_categories.exposure_harmful_substances: Chemical, dust, fume, gas, vapor, radiation exposure
hazard_categories.exposure_extreme_temperatures: Heat stress, cold stress, burns from hot surfaces/materials
hazard_categories.electrical: Shock, arc flash, arc blast, electrocution
hazard_categories.fire_explosion: Ignition sources near flammables, dust explosions, chemical reactions
hazard_categories.oxygen_deficient_enriched: Confined spaces, inert gas purging, oxygen displacement
hazard_categories.noise: Hearing damage from equipment, tools, processes
hazard_categories.ergonomic: Repetitive motion, awkward postures, forceful exertions, vibration
hazard_categories.weather: Lightning, high winds, extreme heat/cold, rain/ice

risk_matrix.description: Tool to prioritize hazards based on severity and probability

severity_levels.1_negligible: First aid or less, no property damage
severity_levels.3_moderate: Lost time injury, moderate property damage, restricted work
severity_levels.4_major: Permanent disability, major property damage, hospitalization
severity_levels.5_catastrophic: Fatality, multiple hospitalizations, major environmental release

probability_levels.1_rare: Very unlikely to occur, no history
probability_levels.2_unlikely: Could happen but not expected, rare occurrence in industry
probability_levels.3_possible: Might occur, has happened in industry
probability_levels.4_likely: Will probably occur, has happened on similar projects
probability_levels.5_almost_certain: Expected to occur, has happened on this project/site

risk_levels.5_to_9_medium: Additional controls required. Supervisor review required.
risk_levels.10_to_15_high: Significant controls required. Management approval required before proceeding.
risk_levels.16_to_25_critical: Work must not proceed without senior management review. May require alternative method, engineered solution, or task redesign.

hierarchy_of_controls.description: NIOSH Hierarchy of Controls — applied in order of effectiveness

examples: Design out the hazard in planning phase
Prefabricate at ground level instead of at height

2_substitution.description: Replace the hazard with something less hazardous
examples: Use water-based solvent instead of flammable solvent
Use mechanical lifting instead of manual
Use pre-fabricated assemblies instead of field-built

4_administrative_controls.effectiveness: Less effective — depends on human behavior
examples: Work scheduling to avoid heat of day

5_PPE.description: Protect the worker with personal protective equipment
5_PPE.effectiveness: Least effective — last line of defense
5_PPE.important_note: PPE should never be the sole control. It must be combined with higher-level controls.

steps: Review scaffold plan and gather materials
key_controls: Red tag until complete and inspected

steps: Set up fire watch and extinguisher
key_controls: Proper PPE (welding hood, FR, gloves, leathers)

key_hazards: Struck by falling materials from edge
Energy release if not properly isolated
key_controls: Attendant at entry point at all times

required_elements: Emergency escape procedures and escape route assignments
Procedures for employees who remain to operate critical plant equipment before evacuating
Procedures to account for all employees after evacuation
Rescue and medical duties for designated employees
Means of reporting fires and other emergencies
Names or regular job titles of persons to contact for further information

alarm_signals.note: Site-specific — must be communicated to ALL workers including contractors during orientation

common_signals.intermittent_horn: Shelter in place (or all clear, site-specific)
common_signals.all_clear: Resume normal operations (typically announced over PA after continuous horn stops)

requirements: Pre-designated assembly areas, marked and communicated
Must be at safe distance from potential hazard areas
Upwind from process areas when possible
Headcount or badge-scan accountability system
Designated person responsible for headcount at each muster point
Must accommodate all personnel in area
Must not block emergency vehicle access

methods: Badge scan (in/out at gate with muster point scan)
T-card system (visual card system at muster point)
accountability.key_rule: NEVER re-enter the work area to search for missing personnel. Report missing persons to emergency coordinator. Only trained rescue teams re-enter.

steps: 3. ONLY attempt to fight fire if: trained, fire is small/contained, safe exit available behind you, correct extinguisher available
4. Evacuate to muster point if fire cannot be quickly controlled
6. Guide emergency responders to fire location

fire_extinguisher_types.class_A: Ordinary combustibles (wood, paper, cloth) — water, foam, dry chemical
fire_extinguisher_types.class_B: Flammable liquids (gasoline, oil, solvents) — CO2, dry chemical, foam
fire_extinguisher_types.class_C: Energized electrical — CO2, dry chemical (never water)
fire_extinguisher_types.class_D: Combustible metals (magnesium, titanium) — special dry powder
fire_extinguisher_types.ABC_multipurpose: Most common on construction sites — covers A, B, and C

PASS_technique.A: Aim at the base of the fire
PASS_technique.S2: Sweep side to side at the base

immediate_actions: 2. Evacuate if material is unknown, toxic, or large quantity
3. If safe and trained: contain spill (absorbent, berms, dam drains)
5. Avoid contact — do not walk through spill
8. Report to NRC (National Response Center, 1-800-424-8802) if reportable quantity released to environment

reportable_quantities.note: Defined per substance in 40 CFR 302 Table 302.4

common_examples.oil_sheen_on_water: Any visible sheen on navigable water (reportable under CWA)

response_steps: 1. Ensure scene is safe before approaching
2. Call for help (911, site emergency number, radio)
3. Begin first aid / CPR if trained (do not move victim unless in danger)
4. AED — apply if cardiac arrest (follow device prompts)
5. Control severe bleeding (direct pressure, tourniquet if trained)
6. Maintain airway (recovery position if unconscious and breathing)
7. Treat for shock (elevate legs if no spinal injury suspected, keep warm)
medical_emergency.first_aid_supplies_location: First aid kits must be in a conspicuous, readily accessible location. AED locations posted. Eye wash stations within 10 seconds travel time where corrosives are used (ANSI Z358.1).

eyewash_requirements.location: Within 10 seconds unobstructed travel from corrosive/chemical hazard
eyewash_requirements.flow: 0.4 GPM for 15 minutes (plumbed) or minimum 15-minute supply (portable)
eyewash_requirements.inspection: Plumbed units activated weekly. Portable units inspected per manufacturer (typical 3-6 months).

rescue_from_heights.requirement: Employer must provide for prompt rescue in the event of a fall (29 CFR 1926.502(d)(20))
methods: Self-rescue (worker can reach structure/platform after fall arrest)
Assisted rescue (co-workers use rescue equipment to reach and lower victim)
Technical rescue (trained rescue team with specialized equipment)
Aerial lift rescue (position aerial lift to retrieve fallen worker)

suspension_trauma.definition: Potentially fatal condition when a worker hangs motionless in a harness — blood pools in legs, reduced cardiac output
suspension_trauma.onset: Can begin within minutes of suspension
prevention_during_suspension: Worker should keep moving legs, push against structure
Suspension trauma straps (built into harness or add-on) — stand in loops to activate leg muscles
Prompt rescue is critical — rescue within 10-15 minutes
suspension_trauma.post_rescue: Do NOT lay victim flat immediately — keep in seated/crouched position. Sudden position change can cause cardiac arrest (reflow syndrome). Gradual transition to lying position. Medical evaluation required.

requirements: Rescue service must be identified before entry
Rescue service must be able to reach victim within a timeframe appropriate for the hazards (consider non-IDLH vs. IDLH scenarios)
On-site rescue team must practice making rescues from representative spaces at least annually
Off-site rescue service must be evaluated for capability and response time
Non-entry rescue (retrieval system) preferred when feasible: full body harness, retrieval line, mechanical retrieval device (tripod/davit)

retrieval_system_requirements.when_required: Required for all permit-required confined space entries unless system increases risk or would not contribute to rescue
retrieval_system_requirements.components: Full body harness with retrieval line attached to chest or back D-ring, mechanical device (tripod/davit arm with winch)
retrieval_system_requirements.attendant_rule: Attendant must NOT enter the space for rescue unless trained as rescuer AND relieved by another attendant

methods: Two-way radios (most common on construction sites — dedicated emergency channel)
Cell phones (backup — may not work in all areas of plant)
Visual alarms (strobe lights for high-noise areas)
emergency_contact_information: 911 (or site-specific emergency number if on-site fire/EMS)

OSHA_10_hour.description: Basic safety awareness for construction workers
topics: Focus Four hazards (falls, struck-by, caught-in/between, electrocution)
OSHA_10_hour.duration: 10 hours (minimum over 2 days, cannot exceed 7.5 hours in one day)
OSHA_10_hour.validity: Does not expire per OSHA (some states, site owners, and unions require renewal every 3-5 years)
OSHA_10_hour.card: DOL card issued through authorized OSHA trainer

OSHA_30_hour.description: Comprehensive safety training for supervisors and safety professionals
topics: All OSHA 10 topics plus expanded content
OSHA_30_hour.duration: 30 hours (minimum over 4 days, cannot exceed 7.5 hours in one day)
OSHA_30_hour.validity: Does not expire per OSHA (renewal practices same as OSHA 10)
OSHA_30_hour.card: DOL card issued through authorized OSHA trainer

fall_protection_training.required_for: All workers exposed to fall hazards of 6 feet or more
content: Nature of fall hazards in the work area
Correct procedures for erecting, maintaining, disassembling, and inspecting fall protection systems
Use and operation of each type of fall protection system used
Role of each employee in the safety monitoring system (if used)
Limitations on use of mechanical equipment during roofing on low-slope roofs
fall_protection_training.frequency: Initial training before exposure. Retraining when: reason to believe employee does not understand, changes in workplace/equipment, or inadequacies in knowledge observed.
fall_protection_training.documentation: Written certification: employee name, date of training, signature of trainer or competent person

scaffold_competent_person.required_for: Persons who inspect, erect, dismantle, or supervise scaffold operations
content: Correct procedures for erecting, disassembling, moving, operating, repairing, inspecting, and maintaining the type of scaffold in use
Design criteria, maximum intended load-carrying capacity, and intended use
scaffold_competent_person.frequency: Initial training. Retraining when: type of scaffold changes, hazards change, or deficiencies in knowledge observed.
scaffold_competent_person.note: No specific OSHA-required 'certification' — competent person is determined by the employer based on knowledge, training, and experience. However, most industrial sites require documented scaffold training from a recognized program.

content: Proper use of equipment (PPE, monitoring, ventilation, communication, lighting)
authorized_entrant.frequency: Before first entry, refresher annually or when conditions change

content: What to do if unauthorized person approaches or enters
attendant.frequency: Before first assignment as attendant, refresher annually

content: All entrant and attendant training plus:
How to determine if entry conditions are acceptable
How to verify controls are in place
When to cancel or terminate entry
Use of rescue equipment (retrieval systems, SCBA/SAR)
Practice making rescues from representative permit spaces at least annually
rescue_team.frequency: Annual practice rescue from representative space, ongoing competency

requirements: Qualified by third-party evaluation or employer-qualified
Must know and understand standard hand signals (ASME B30.5)
Knowledge of various signal methods (hand, voice, electronic)
Demonstrated competence through evaluation
crane_signal_person.frequency: Initial qualification. Re-evaluation if observed deficiencies.

components: Evaluation by employer in the workplace
forklift.frequency: Initial certification. Evaluation every 3 years. Retraining if: unsafe operation observed, involvement in accident/near-miss, different truck type, or workplace conditions change.
forklift.note: Operator-specific AND equipment-specific — certification on one type does not qualify for all types

training_content: Workplace hazards (power lines, overhead obstructions, uneven ground, wind)
aerial_lift.frequency: Initial training before operation. Retraining as needed. Many sites require annual refresher.

first_aid_cpr_aed.osha_reference: 29 CFR 1926.50 (requires availability of trained first aider where no medical facility in near proximity)
first_aid_cpr_aed.training_standards: American Red Cross, American Heart Association, or equivalent
first_aid_cpr_aed.certification_duration: Typically 2 years for first aid, 2 years for CPR/AED
first_aid_cpr_aed.site_requirement: At least one trained person per crew or work area. Many sites require all supervisors to be trained.

h2s_awareness.required_for: All workers in refineries, petrochemical plants, and areas with potential H2S exposure
content: Properties of H2S (colorless, heavier than air, smells like rotten eggs at low concentration, deadens sense of smell at higher concentration)
Exposure limits (10 ppm TWA OSHA PEL, 20 ppm ceiling, 50 ppm 10-min peak)
Location of wind indicators and escape routes (crosswind/upwind)

key_h2s_thresholds.10_ppm: OSHA PEL (8-hour TWA). Alarm setpoint on most monitors.
key_h2s_thresholds.15_ppm: OSHA STEL (15-minute). Common first alarm on personal monitors.
key_h2s_thresholds.20_ppm: OSHA ceiling — do not exceed at any time
key_h2s_thresholds.50_ppm: 10-minute maximum peak. Olfactory fatigue begins (cannot smell it).
key_h2s_thresholds.100_ppm: IDLH (Immediately Dangerous to Life or Health). Loss of smell. Can be fatal in minutes to hours.
key_h2s_thresholds.300_plus_ppm: Rapid loss of consciousness, death possible within minutes

h2s_awareness.frequency: Before initial assignment. Refresher annually or per site requirements.

program_requirements: Medical evaluation (questionnaire/physician review) before fit testing
Fit testing annually for each make/model/size of tight-fitting respirator
Training on proper use, limitations, maintenance, storage
Clean shaven where respirator seals to face (no stubble, beards, sideburns that interfere with seal)

fit_test_methods.qualitative: Pass/fail using test agent (Bitrex, saccharin, irritant smoke). For half-mask only.
fit_test_methods.quantitative: Uses instrument to measure face seal leakage. Required for full-face respirators. Minimum fit factor: 100 for half-mask, 500 for full-face.

respiratory_protection.frequency: Medical evaluation before initial use. Fit test annually. Retraining annually.

content: Globally Harmonized System (GHS) for classification and labeling of chemicals
Reading and understanding Safety Data Sheets (16 sections)
GHS label elements: product identifier, signal word (Danger/Warning), hazard statements, pictograms, precautionary statements, supplier identification
Physical and health hazards of chemicals in the workplace

ghs_pictograms.flame: Flammable, self-reactive, pyrophoric, self-heating, emits flammable gas, organic peroxide
ghs_pictograms.corrosion: Corrosive to metals, skin corrosion, serious eye damage
ghs_pictograms.exclamation_mark: Irritant, skin sensitizer, acute toxicity (harmful), narcotic effects, respiratory tract irritation
ghs_pictograms.health_hazard: Carcinogen, respiratory sensitizer, reproductive toxicity, target organ toxicity, mutagenicity, aspiration hazard

hazcom_ghs.frequency: Initial training for all workers. Retraining when new hazards introduced or when understanding is deficient.

arc_flash_nfpa_70e.standard: NFPA 70E Standard for Electrical Safety in the Workplace
arc_flash_nfpa_70e.required_for: All workers who may be exposed to electrical hazards (qualified and unqualified persons)

qualified_persons: Shock and arc flash hazard awareness
Arc flash PPE categories and selection
Use of insulated tools and test equipment
unqualified_persons: What to do if someone contacts energized equipment

arc_flash_nfpa_70e.frequency: Initial training. Retraining at frequency determined by employer (typically annually or every 3 years). NFPA 70E requires retraining at intervals not exceeding 3 years.

loto_training.osha_reference: 29 CFR 1910.147 (General Industry, commonly applied in construction) and 29 CFR 1926.417 (Construction electrical LOTO)

roles.authorized_employee: Applies locks/tags, performs the work. Full LOTO training required.
roles.affected_employee: Works in area where LOTO is applied but does not perform service/maintenance. Awareness training required.
roles.other_employee: May work near LOTO. Awareness of LOTO purpose and prohibition against removing locks/tags.

content: Purpose of LOTO and types of hazardous energy
Proper use of locks, tags, and lockout devices
loto_training.frequency: Initial training before performing LOTO. Retraining when: procedures change, new equipment, periodic inspection reveals inadequacy. Annual inspection of LOTO procedures required.

what_to_document: Method of training (classroom, OJT, video, etc.)
Written test or practical evaluation results (if applicable)

retention.general_rule: Duration of employment plus 30 years for exposure records (1910.1020). Most sites maintain training records for duration of project plus 3-5 years.

contractor_safety_management.description: Owner/operator program to manage safety of contractors performing work on their site

prequalification: EMR review (typically require below 1.0, some below 0.80)
TRIR review (typically require below industry average, often below 1.0)
OSHA citation history (check OSHA online database)
orientation: Site-specific safety orientation for all personnel before badge issuance
Duration typically 2-8 hours (some large projects have multi-day orientations)
Covers: site hazards, emergency procedures, permit systems, PPE requirements, prohibited actions, reporting procedures
Written test and/or badge issuance upon completion
ongoing_management: Regular safety audits of contractor work areas
Weekly safety statistics reporting from contractors
Consequence management (warnings, stand-downs, removal from site)

short_service_employee_SSE.description: Program to identify and provide additional oversight for new or inexperienced workers
short_service_employee_SSE.typical_criteria: Less than 6 months experience in the specific trade or on the specific site (some programs use 6 months to 1 year)
short_service_employee_SSE.identification: Green hard hat, sticker on hard hat, or badge marking to visually identify SSE workers
requirements: Assigned to experienced mentor/buddy
Cannot work alone
Extra supervision and monitoring
Additional JSA review before starting tasks
Typically limited SSE percentage on a crew (common: no more than 20-25% of crew can be SSE)
Competency sign-off before advancing out of SSE status

behavior_based_safety_BBS.description: Safety approach focusing on observable behaviors rather than attitudes or conditions
process: 1. Identify critical safety behaviors through incident data and JSA review
2. Develop observation checklist targeting those behaviors
3. Train observers (typically peer observers, not just supervisors)
4. Conduct regular observations (in the field, during actual work)
5. Provide immediate positive or corrective feedback
6. Collect and analyze data to identify trends
7. Implement improvements based on data
8. Celebrate improvements and recognize safe behaviors
key_principles: Non-punitive — focus on coaching, not discipline
Peer-to-peer observations are more effective than top-down
Positive reinforcement of safe behaviors is primary tool
Data-driven — track at-risk behaviors and trending
Worker engagement and participation is essential

stop_work_authority_SWA.description: Every person on site has the right and obligation to stop work when they observe an unsafe condition or act
key_elements: Universal — applies to every person regardless of employer, rank, or trade
No reprisal — workers cannot be disciplined for exercising SWA in good faith
Immediate — work stops immediately upon SWA exercise
Resolution before restart — work does not resume until the concern is resolved and all affected workers are satisfied
Documentation — SWA exercises should be documented and tracked (as a leading indicator)
process: 1. Observe unsafe condition or act
2. Stop the work immediately
3. Notify the workers and supervisor
4. Explain the concern
5. Correct the condition
6. Get agreement from all involved parties that it is safe to proceed
7. Resume work
8. Document the SWA exercise

safety_observation_program.description: Structured program for identifying safe and at-risk behaviors and conditions in the field
observation_card_elements: Category (PPE, body position, housekeeping, tools/equipment, procedures, etc.)

toolbox_talk_program.description: Short safety meetings held at the work area, typically daily before shift or before starting a new task
toolbox_talk_program.frequency: Daily (most sites), or before each new task
content_sources: Planned topic relevant to the day's work
Recent incidents or near-misses (site or industry)
Seasonal hazards (heat stress, cold stress, storms)
Permit review for the day's work
JSA review for the day's tasks

types.pre_employment: Before starting work on site. Required by most industrial sites.
types.random: Unannounced selection from workforce. Common frequency: 25-50% of workforce annually.
types.post_incident: After any recordable injury, vehicle accident, or near-miss (per site policy). Must be conducted within reasonable time (typically 2-8 hours).
types.reasonable_suspicion: When trained supervisor observes signs of impairment. Requires two trained supervisors to agree (in many programs).
types.return_to_duty: After a positive test and completion of treatment program
types.follow_up: Unannounced testing after return to duty (typically 6 directly observed tests in 12 months)

panel.expanded_panel: May include: benzodiazepines, barbiturates, methadone, propoxyphene, expanded opioids
panel.alcohol: Breath test (BAC). 0.02% or above is typically a positive result on industrial sites. 0.04% DOT threshold.

drug_alcohol_testing.consequences: Varies by site — typically immediate removal from site for positive result. Some programs allow single chance with treatment program (SAP evaluation per DOT rules). Refusal to test = treated as positive.

fit_for_duty.description: Program ensuring workers are physically and mentally fit to safely perform their duties
elements: Pre-task self-assessment (fatigue, medication, illness, emotional state)
Supervisor observation for signs of impairment
Fatigue management (hours of work limitations — many sites enforce 12-hour shift max, 6 day work week max, mandatory rest periods)
Medical fitness-for-duty evaluations for specific tasks (crane operators, confined space entrants, respiratory protection users)

fatigue_management_common_rules.maximum_shift: 12 hours (portal to portal, including travel in some programs)
fatigue_management_common_rules.consecutive_days: Maximum 14 days without a day off (some sites limit to 6-7 days)
fatigue_management_common_rules.overtime_approval: Required for exceeding normal shift limits

SWPPP.regulatory_basis: Clean Water Act (CWA), 40 CFR 122, EPA Construction General Permit (CGP)
SWPPP.when_required: Construction activity disturbing 1 acre or more of land (or part of a common plan of development that will disturb 1 acre or more)
key_elements: Site description and map showing drainage, receiving waters, disturbed areas
Description of BMPs (Best Management Practices) to minimize pollutant discharge
Erosion controls (silt fences, erosion blankets, temporary seeding, mulching)
Sediment controls (sediment basins, silt fences, inlet protection)
Good housekeeping practices (spill prevention, waste management, material storage)
Inspection schedule (every 7 days and within 24 hours of 0.25-inch rain event)
Corrective action procedures
Pollution prevention for specific activities (concrete washout, paint, fuel, chemicals)

inspection_requirements.frequency: Every 7 calendar days OR every 14 days with rain event inspections within 24 hours of 0.25-inch rainfall
inspection_requirements.inspector: Qualified person (competent in SWPPP and erosion/sediment controls)
inspection_requirements.documentation: Inspection reports maintained on site with SWPPP

SPCC.full_name: Spill Prevention, Control, and Countermeasure Plan
SPCC.when_required: Facility with total aboveground oil storage capacity exceeding 1,320 gallons (or underground capacity exceeding 42,000 gallons), with reasonable expectation of discharge to navigable waters
key_elements: Facility description and oil storage inventory
Potential spill scenarios and discharge predictions
Containment and diversionary structures (berms, dikes, holding 110% of largest container)
Inspection and monitoring procedures
Personnel training
Security measures to prevent unauthorized access/vandalism
Spill response procedures and equipment
Contact information for cleanup contractors and agencies
SPCC.construction_site_application: If construction project stores more than 1,320 gallons of oil products (diesel, hydraulic fluid, lubricants, etc.) in aggregate, SPCC plan or equivalent may be required.

hazardous_waste_characteristics.ignitability: Flash point below 140F, or can cause fire through friction/absorption/chemical change
hazardous_waste_characteristics.corrosivity: pH less than or equal to 2, or greater than or equal to 12.5
hazardous_waste_characteristics.reactivity: Unstable, reacts violently with water, generates toxic gases, capable of detonation
hazardous_waste_characteristics.toxicity: Contains listed toxic substances above regulatory levels (TCLP test)

generator_categories.VSQG: Very Small Quantity Generator — less than 220 lbs/month
generator_categories.LQG: Large Quantity Generator — more than 2,200 lbs/month

construction_site_common_hazardous_waste: Used oil (regulated but not hazardous if managed properly)
Contaminated soil from excavation in brownfield areas

key_rules: Proper labeling: 'Hazardous Waste' label with contents, accumulation start date
Containers closed except when adding/removing waste
Compatible waste only in same container
Storage time limits: SQG 270 days (or 180 days for shipment under 200 miles), LQG 90 days

construction_site_concerns: Dust/particulate matter (PM10, PM2.5) from earthwork, demolition, material handling
VOCs from painting, coating, adhesives, solvents
Silica dust from concrete cutting, grinding, drilling (29 CFR 1926.1153)
Asbestos from demolition/renovation of older structures

silica_standard.table_1: Specifies engineering controls, work practices, and respiratory protection by task (e.g., concrete cutting, grinding, drilling)
requirements: Follow Table 1 controls OR conduct exposure monitoring
Written exposure control plan
Medical surveillance for workers exposed above action level for 30+ days/year
Training on silica hazards

refinery_specific: Benzene monitoring near process units (PEL: 1 ppm TWA, STEL: 5 ppm)
H2S monitoring (continuous monitors, personal monitors in process areas)
VOC monitoring during line breaks, vessel openings
Combustible gas monitoring in classified areas

noise_monitoring_hearing_conservation.osha_reference: 29 CFR 1926.52 and 29 CFR 1926.101 (Construction), 29 CFR 1910.95 (General Industry program, often applied)
program_elements: Noise monitoring (area surveys and/or personal dosimetry)
Hearing protection provided at 85 dBA and above (required at 90 dBA if engineering controls not feasible)
Baseline audiogram within 6 months of first exposure at or above 85 dBA TWA (within 1 year if mobile test van used)
Standard Threshold Shift (STS) evaluation: 10 dB average shift at 2000, 3000, and 4000 Hz in either ear
Hearing protector selection (NRR rating — effective NRR = (NRR - 7) / 2 for real-world derating)
Training on noise hazards, hearing protection use, and audiometric testing purpose

heat_stress_program.osha_guidance: OSHA Recommended Practices (no specific construction standard, enforced under General Duty Clause). OSHA National Emphasis Program on Heat began 2022.

WBGT_monitoring.description: Wet Bulb Globe Temperature — measures heat stress accounting for temperature, humidity, wind, and radiant heat
WBGT_monitoring.note: WBGT meters should be placed at the work location (radiant heat from equipment can significantly affect reading)

program_elements: WBGT or heat index monitoring when temperatures warrant
Water — cool water available, encourage 1 cup every 15-20 minutes
Rest — scheduled rest breaks in shade/cool area (rest frequency increases with heat)
Shade — shaded or climate-controlled rest areas provided

Acclimatization — new or returning workers (after 2+ weeks away) require gradual exposure: start at 20% of full duration on day 1, increase 20% per day for new workers; 50% on day 1, increase 20% per day for returning workers
Training — recognition of heat illness symptoms, buddy system, emergency procedures
Emergency procedures — how to respond to heat illness, when to call 911

heat_illness_types.heat_rash: Red bumpy rash with itching. Move to cooler area, keep area dry.
heat_illness_types.heat_cramps: Muscle spasms/cramps. Stop activity, drink water, rest in cool area.
heat_illness_types.heat_exhaustion: Heavy sweating, weakness, nausea, dizziness, cool/moist skin, fast pulse. Move to cool area, loosen clothing, cool the worker, give water. Medical attention if no improvement.
heat_illness_types.heat_stroke: HIGH BODY TEMPERATURE (above 103F), red/hot/dry skin, rapid strong pulse, confusion, loss of consciousness. MEDICAL EMERGENCY — call 911 immediately, move to cool area, cool the worker aggressively (water, ice, fans). Do NOT give fluids to unconscious person.

elements: Warm, sweet beverages available (no alcohol or caffeine)
Layered clothing — inner wicking layer, insulating layer, outer wind/waterproof layer
Work/warm-up schedule based on wind chill
Training on recognition of hypothermia and frostbite symptoms

conditions.frostbite: Numbness, tingling, stinging, aching in extremities (fingers, toes, nose, ears). White or grayish-yellow skin. Do NOT rub affected area. Immerse in warm (not hot) water.
conditions.hypothermia: Shivering, exhaustion, confusion, fumbling hands, memory loss, slurred speech. Core body temperature below 95F. MEDICAL EMERGENCY — call 911, move to warm area, remove wet clothing, warm the center of the body.

daily_area_inspections.who: Supervisors, foremen, and designated safety personnel
daily_area_inspections.when: Before each shift begins and continuously throughout the shift
what_to_inspect: Housekeeping (clear walkways, organized work area, waste properly disposed)
Fall protection in place and used correctly
Barricades and warning signs in place
Tool condition (guards in place, no damage)
daily_area_inspections.documentation: Daily inspection form or checklist. Deficiencies documented with corrective actions.

weekly_safety_walkdowns.who: Safety manager, superintendent, client safety representative
weekly_safety_walkdowns.purpose: Broader inspection of work areas, focus on trends, program compliance, and conditions not caught in daily inspections
typical_checklist_areas: SWPPP BMPs in place and effective
Emergency equipment (eyewash, first aid, AEDs, fire extinguishers)

requirements.when: Before each work shift by competent person. After any event that could affect structural integrity (weather, impact, modification).
what_to_check: All connections secure — pins, bolts, braces
All planks in place, secured, proper overhang (6-12 inches)
Guardrails at 38-45 inches, midrails in place
Toeboards in place (3.5 inches minimum height)
No gaps greater than 1 inch between planks
Access ladder/stair tower in place and secure
Ties to building/structure at required intervals (per design)
Tag updated with current date and inspector name

items: Control functions — test all controls for proper operation
